title: Publications
text: My work spans associative memory, computational models of symbolic behaviour, reinforcement learning, adaptive recommendation, and large-scale health data analysis.

title: Publications
text:

title: Research
text: My work combines formal computational modelling with applied behavioural data science. The projects below show how that agenda travels across theory, experimentation, and real-world data.

title: Research
text:
Retail Fresh

Behavioural and context-aware modelling in retail systems
In the Retail Fresh project, I lead modelling work on retail demand and sales dynamics with the goal of supporting better forecasting and reducing food waste. The research combines machine learning with behavioural regularities in real purchasing environments, where weekly rhythms, holidays, local events, product-level patterns, and environmental factors all shape demand.
Retail Fresh is an externally funded Research Council of Norway R&D project with Link Retail as the project owner. My work is connected to the Machine Learning Group at UiB and focuses on how forecasting models can support operational decision making in food retail.
Role: Machine learning researcher and lead modeller
Methods: Forecasting, hierarchical time-series models, AutoML benchmarking, operational evaluation
Focus: Applied AI, interpretable forecasting, behavioural regularities in retail data
Current direction: Comparing direct, hierarchical, and automated forecasting approaches for bread demand and related retail categories, with attention to practical deployment and evaluation.
Funding page
Project overview in the Machine Learning Group
Link Retail
Retail Fresh bread forecasting repository

title: Research
text:
Associative Memory Models

Clique- and tournament-based associative memories
This line of work grows out of my PhD in Informatics and focuses on clique-based, nested-clique, and tournament-based associative memory architectures. These models are rooted in graph theory and coding theory, and they study how structured information can be stored, organised, and retrieved in scalable systems.
I am interested in associative memory not only as a neural-inspired mechanism, but as a way of thinking about representation itself: how information becomes structured, how sequences are preserved, and how retrieval can remain interpretable as systems scale.
Role: Researcher, model developer, proposal writer
Methods: Graph theory, coding theory, simulation, neural associative memory
Focus: Structured representation, sequence storage, theoretical AI
Current direction: Extending clique-tournament architectures and developing new proposals around memory structure and scalable recall.
PhD thesis
Tournament-based sequence storage repository

title: Research
text:
Computational Behaviour and Projective Simulation

Adaptive learning, stimulus equivalence, and reinforcement
This research strand centres on computational models of adaptive behaviour, especially Projective Simulation, Equivalence Projective Simulation (EPS), and Enhanced-EPS. These models provide a computational lens on learning dynamics, generalisation, and the emergence of stimulus equivalence.
The same strand also includes work on contrastive reinforcement learning and on adaptive recommendation through the Balanced Difficulty Task Finder, where behavioural theory, user state, and feedback are used to calibrate challenge and engagement.
Role: PhD researcher, collaborator, supervisor
Methods: Reinforcement learning, projective simulation, contrastive learning, behavioural modelling
Focus: Learning dynamics, symbolic behaviour, adaptive systems
Current direction: New simulation frameworks that combine reinforcement and contrastive objectives, plus ongoing work on reject relations and quantum projective simulation.
OsloMet PhD thesis
EPS code repository
E-EPS code repository
Balanced Difficulty Task Finder paper

title: Research
text:
Prescription History and Registry Data

Longitudinal prescription trajectories and Parkinson’s disease
As a postdoctoral researcher at UiB, and now through continued supervision and collaboration, I work with Norwegian prescription and patient registry data to study longitudinal trajectories associated with Parkinson’s disease and related outcomes.
These data are especially interesting because they are administrative at scale while also reflecting real-world decision processes over time. The modelling challenges include temporal representation, confounding, graph-based drug relationships, and interpretability in settings where domain understanding matters.
Role: Postdoctoral researcher, supervisor, collaborator
Methods: Representation learning, temporal modelling, graph-based modelling, longitudinal analysis
Focus: Health data, prescription trajectories, interpretable modelling
Current direction: Prescription-data pipelines, graph representations of drug histories, and supervised student projects on feature design, balancing strategies, and embedding-based analysis.
DRONE project page

title: Research
text:
Digital Behaviour and NLP

Online discourse, digital traces, and responsible AI
My work on digital trace data explores how algorithms and online environments shape behaviour. This includes research on hate speech detection and discourse mapping in Norwegian social media, as well as broader interests in feedback loops, exposure dynamics, and responsible modelling in platform settings.
This area connects NLP workflows with behavioural data science and raises important questions about ethics, interpretability, and the long-term effects of adaptive systems.
Role: Assistant professor and researcher
Methods: NLP, BERT-based models, text analysis, digital trace analysis
Focus: Online behaviour, public discourse, responsible AI
Mapping Hateful Utterances project page
Hate speech report

title: Research
text:
Connecting Themes
Across these projects, a few ideas recur:

learning as adaptation under feedback
representation as a structured and interpretable object
behaviour as something that unfolds over time and context
machine learning as most useful when paired with theory, evaluation, and domain understanding

That combination of formal models and applied data work is the thread that ties together my academic profile.

title: Teaching
text: My teaching is grounded in adaptive learning, structured feedback, and transparent reasoning. I aim to help students move from technical execution to independent scientific judgment by making expectations clear, scaffolding difficult tasks, and treating writing and method choice as part of the research process.

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching Philosophy
I design teaching and supervision around a simple principle: students learn deeply when challenge is clear, support is structured, and reasoning is made visible.
This perspective is closely connected to my research on adaptive learning. In practice, it means calibrating tasks so they are demanding but still achievable, building confidence without lowering standards. In data-intensive subjects, I emphasise reproducibility, method justification, and the relationship between technical choices and defensible scientific claims.

title: Teaching
text:
Assessment and Academic Development
My assessment and committee roles include oral examination, PhD trial lecture assessment, MSc thesis defenses, and participation in recruitment processes. These responsibilities have sharpened my interest in aligning learning goals, evaluation, and academic communication.
I also work actively with AI-aware pedagogy. I encourage students to use new tools responsibly while documenting AI-assisted steps clearly and preserving core academic competencies such as reasoning, writing, explanation, and reproducibility.

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching Interests
I am especially motivated by teaching and supervision in:

machine learning and reinforcement learning
behavioural data science
computational modelling of brain and memory
reproducible analysis workflows
project-based student research

title: Teaching
text:
Educational Contribution
I see teaching as a collaborative and evolving academic responsibility. My goal is to contribute to strong learning environments at both BSc and MSc levels, especially where technical methods, behavioural questions, and independent research practice meet.

title: Talks & Service
text:
Research Development
I am actively involved in proposal writing and research development, including work on:

TRI-CLIQUE, an NFR-oriented proposal on associative memory models
Q-PS-Cog, an ERC-oriented proposal on quantum-enhanced projective simulation for learning and cognition

These efforts extend my broader aim of connecting theoretical AI, behavioural science, and applied data-driven systems.

title: Home
text:
Research Profile
I develop computational models that connect learning mechanisms, structured representation, and real-world behavioural data. A consistent theme across my work is the search for models that are both powerful and interpretable: models that can capture complex adaptive behaviour while still offering theoretical insight.
Across theory and application, my work moves across three linked levels:


Formal models of memory and structure
Graph-based and brain-inspired associative memory models rooted in coding theory, sequence storage, and scalable retrieval.


Adaptive learning and reinforcement
Projective Simulation, EPS/E-EPS, contrastive reinforcement learning, and computational accounts of generalisation and stimulus equivalence.


Behavioural data in applied settings
Machine learning for retail, health registries, and digital traces, with emphasis on temporal structure, interpretability, and responsible AI.

title: Home
text:
Academic Background

PhD in Informatics, University of Bergen: Clique-Based Neural Associative Memories
PhD in Behavior Analysis, OsloMet: When Behavior Analysis Meets Machine Learning: Formation of Stimulus Equivalence Classes and Adaptive Learning in Artificial Agents

title: Home
text:
Research Agenda
I am especially interested in building a cohesive programme around three themes: structured and neuro-symbolic intelligent systems, behavioural modelling in real-world contexts, and long-term feedback dynamics in adaptive digital environments. Across these themes, I aim to strengthen interpretability, robustness, and evaluation in behavioural data science and trustworthy AI.

title: Home
text:
Collaboration
I welcome collaborations across artificial intelligence, machine learning, behavioural science, cognitive modelling, recommender systems, and longitudinal data analysis. If your work touches learning, memory, structured representation, adaptive systems, or behaviour-rich data, there is a good chance we have something to talk about.
